speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Mr. Nathan Offen on August the 11th, 1996. Mr. Offen, did you ever return to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I returned to Poland. You see, I always promised Helen that one day I will go back to Poland and find the people where she stayed who saved her life during the war. Helen didn't-- couldn't read or write. And she didn't remember even the names or-- or much where-- where it was. But I said I will become a detective and find-- find-- find the people because I have clues. I know where to look for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in nine Helen was-- was sick already. Helen was sick with cancer already, for a few years already, about seven years already. And-- and she wasn't getting any better. So I decided it's time to go. So we boarded the plane, and there was opportunity because the Cracow Society where I am a member of was going to Poland. So I said I will join them and go on my own, take off some time and go on my own and-- and locate the-- the people and the place where Helen stayed during the war. Anyhow, we hired a taxi in Cracow and we drove out to-- to Olesno-- to Olesno and Dombrowa. And what Helen told me this story about, my only clue was that the fellow was a tailor. So I decided I will go from house to house and first question would be, ask the people how long they living here? And if they told me that they, oh, they've been living here for so many years, I didn't bother. I wanted to know only to talk to the people who stay here during the war. And fortunately, in going checking, I found some people who said they were during the war. And so my second question was, I asked, do you know a tailor? And they may start mention names. And I wanted to hear the sound from Helen. Does it sound familiar? She couldn't remember. So we went to visit the tailor. It wasn't the party.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went to another house. And she said, sure, she knows a tailor. He's on that street. And we rode with a taxi. And there was-- we took another friend of our friends who lives in Portland, who is an attorney. I forgot her name. And we rode to that house. As soon we approach, there was a-- a church. And Helen said, yes. I know this place. This is the church where I used to go. And she gave direction to the taxi driver where to go. And we drove into that place. We drove into that yard of the place. And there comes a woman, who we couldn't see the face. She was maybe five feet or less. She looks like the Hunchback of Notre Dame with a little cane, her head facing the ground, and she stooping like that. And Helen goes into that yard, and she looks at her. And that woman yelled out, Helenka! Her name in Polish was Helenka. And she yelled out. She recognized her. And this was such an emotional experience that this is just unbelievable. And then we went into the house. Then her husband came in, who was the tailor. Now, the farmer who took Helen in was dead already a number of years already. Now, she was the-- the daughter-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the farmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the farmer. And then I met the son, who was the tailor. And was a very emotional experience getting together. As a matter of fact, I taped everything. But everything is in Polish, which would be difficult to. [SIGHS] And we record everything. And then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] then-- there-- there are little things that I is not worth it to mention. I mean, she was in a very bad state. I wanted her to go to the doctor. I gave her some money. I wanted to help. And-- and eventually, we had to say goodbye and everything. And we were-- we were-- I had the intention of bringing them over here. But starting talking to some people, she was an ambulatory person. It was very difficult. She wouldn't be able to stand the flight and the excitement. It wasn't-- wasn't advised, even. And when we came back, they honor us in the synagogue. I made a speech about our trips and everything. And-- and now, we started to send packages with food, with clothing to them. And then Helen wasn't getting any better. Eventually, we were going to-- I started procedure with-- I contacted Yad Vashem. I wanted to make the-- put the name on-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Avenue of the Righteous [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Avenue of the Righteousness, I wanted. And I started the procedure. And the Yad Vashem wanted additional information. And Helen wasn't getting better. And in the meantime, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Helen passed away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In-- in January, it's going to be six years. And since I couldn't continue with the procedure, I dropped the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and a few weeks later after Helen died, she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. the daughter-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And this was the end of the-- and I-- I don't even write to them anymore. It's-- it's just too depressing. And I dropped all the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --contacts with him. But I was still continue to writing to one of her daughters. I was writing maybe for four or five years. Oh. There was-- she has a cousin. She gave me a picture blessed from the Pope that I should bring it to Massachusetts that she has a cousin. And somehow, when we came back, I sent that picture to Massachusetts with a letter explaining who I am and whatever. And I got together with those people in Massachusetts, with the cousins. And we decided that we'd be cousins to each other. And we've been writing letters. And very, very friendly, we've been invited to the house a couple of times. And we are in contact. So we are cousins. So I have cousins now in-- in-- in Massachusetts. So-- but since Helen passed away, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --just everything is [SIGHS] coming to an end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm What are some of the things you've been doing in your retirement to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in my retirement, I've been doing, as you see, my paintings are here. I started to paint a couple of years ago, which I enjoy very much. And I also have my hobby where I enjoy playing tennis. I won a trophy already. And I am enjoying life, whatever I have remained, a few years. I like to do traveling. I-- I purchased a condo in Florida, which I intend to spend my winters in Florida. And I met a very nice woman, Gloria Lark, who I am-- who I am living with now. And we enjoy each other's company. We travel together, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure you spend time with your children and grandchildren. You have a daughter on the West Coast?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I enjoy going to my children. Zachary my middle grandson, I visit him on the West Coast. We have a lot in common. He is very much interested in aviation. He wants to be a flier. He's a good soccer player. I play soccer with him. I play tennis with him. And there is-- this is Zachary on the West Coast. And my son-in-law, my son-in-law Barry and my daughter, my oldest daughter Ruth, she has an art gallery on the San Juan Islands, which she's very successful. And I enjoy visiting with her. Then is my middle daughter Gail and her grandson-- her son and my grandson is Jason. He's very athletic, very bright, very bright. He spend with us last winter, he spend a week with us in Florida, which I enjoy having him very much. Then I have my youngest granddaughter, Veronica, the parents Suzanne and Russell Wolf. She's very bright. She's very artistic, very articulate. She likes art. And the biggest joy was when she introduced me to one of her friend, my grandpa the artist. I was six foot tall when she introduced me as my grandpa the artist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she likes to do art with me. We always manage to do some-- I ask her what project she would like to do. And she always mentions she'd like to paint.

speaker: interviewer
text: One last question, Mr. Offen. Why did you decide to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what prompt me to tell the story is because I see the hate, destruction, what's going in this world, and denial that there never was a Holocaust, that it never happened. This is a fabrication by the Zionists, by the Jews, whatever. So it has to be told. It has to be told the truth. The people have to learn. And beside, in another few years, none of us is going to be left. And there was at least it be documented. And you have to learn to love your neighbor, your friends, your everything. Hate is self-destruction. It will just drag you down like a noose around your neck. So we have to learn to respect each other and to love each other. We don't have to love each other, but we have to respect each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Offen.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my parents, Jakob Offen and Gusta [? Rachma ?] [? Gitl ?] Offen. They were married approximately after the second-- after the First World War in Cracow around 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture in Cracow ghetto. This is the beginning of the war where we had to wear armbands identified on the right arm. All the Jews had to wear them. This is a picture with two of my friends. I don't recall the name the fellow in the center. He was a close friend, but I cannot remember his name. The one on mine right is-- he lives in New Jersey. His name is Henry Goldberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am on my left. I am on my left. I am about approximately 17 years old at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you give your name again, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Nathan Offen, and I am on the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: What you see, this is my picture of my sister Miriam. She was approximately 14 years old when she was killed in Majdanek or Belzec in 1941. In the background, you see the birth. This was her birth certificate in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have plans for these documents?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this picture-- this picture was taken. A friend of mine send me when I lived in London in 1946. He sent me from Poland. This picture was taking from the [INAUDIBLE], which means the German document. This is a reproduction from-- form that picture, from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have plans for the documents you do have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would-- I would-- I would like to leave all my documents to my three grandchildren, which I hope that they'd be able one of these days to publish a book or something on-- on our story of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is the picture of myself and my wife, Helen. This was taken approximately 1970.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you give you last name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nathan Offen and Helen Offen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture, what you see of the wedding of my youngest daughter, Suzanne, Dr.-- Dr. Suzanne Offen. From the left what you see the first picture, this is Gail, Gail Offen Kelpfisz. In the back-- in the back is her husband Jeff. Then next to it-- next to in the front row is myself, Nathan Offen. In the back of me is-- is Randy Samuel. This is-- and the next to Samuel, to Randy, is Bernard, my youngest brother. In front of Bernard is Gail. This is Randy's wife. This is my niece. Next to Gail is-- is Hilda Goldberg, Helen's aunt and uncle, Harry and Hilda Goldberg. Then in the center of the picture is the bride, Suzanne, Suzanne Offen Wolf. In the back of her is Russell, her husband. And next to Russell in front is Helen Offen, my wife. Next to Helen in front is-- is Haila, my sister-in-law. In the back of Haila is Sam, my brother, Sam Offen. Next to Sam is-- is Jerry, Jerry-- Jerry Offen, my-- my nephew, which is Sam's son. In front of her is my oldest daughter, Ruth. And this is the whole picture from the wedding. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was approximately 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you see, this is a picture of my three daughters. This is at the wedding of Suzanne. Suzanne is sitting down. This is the bride. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give a full name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Suzanne Offen Wolf. And in the back is-- to my left is Ruth, my oldest daughter, Ruth Pearson Offen. And next to her is Gail Offen Kelpfisz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture that appeared in the Jersey paper after we returned from Portland in May of 1989. This is we went to Poland to find the people who saved Helen during the war. This is what you see on the left. This is the tailor, the son of the person who saved Helen. In the center is Helen. And on the right is his-- the tailor's wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you give Helen's full name, please, including her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen-- this is-- in the center is Helen Goldberg. And-- and this is on the-- Helen Goldberg, who were saved by a-- by the father of the tailor, who is on mine, Mr. Szadowics.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in-- in-- in Dombrowa, Tarnow, Olesno.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you give Helen's married name, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen's married name below, you see the picture of Helen and I in my house in Paramus. I lived in Paramus at the time at 39 Hampshire Road.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is Helen Goldberg Offen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen Goldberg Offen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest grandson, Jason Kelpfisz Offen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is my middle grandson. This is Zachary Offen Pearson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is my youngest granddaughter, Veronica Offen Pearson. I hope that one of my grandchildren I'm going to leave him all the documentation. I hope that one day they can follow my story right or whatever they want to do. But all the document is going to be left to them after I am deceased. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
